questions.question: What is this course about?
questions.answer: This course is a rigorous, laboratory-intensive introduction to forensic science for high school students. It covers 18 chapters across six thematic modules: forensic infrastructure and crime scene methodology; physical and microscopic trace evidence; biological evidence and biochemistry; chemical and biomolecular analysis; anatomical and ecological analysis; and digital evidence including facial recognition, cell phone analytics, and social media analysis.

questions.question: Who is this course for?
questions.answer: This course is designed for high school students in grades 9–12 with introductory biology and chemistry backgrounds. It is appropriate for college-prep and AP-track students, and is also suitable for dual-enrollment programs at community colleges.

questions.question: What prerequisites do I need to take this course?
questions.answer: Students should have completed introductory biology (cell structure and genetics basics), introductory chemistry (states of matter, chemical reactions, lab safety), and Algebra I (ratio and proportion calculations). Basic trigonometry is helpful but not required.

questions.question: How is this textbook organized?
questions.answer: The textbook is organized into 18 chapters across six thematic modules, progressing from legal foundations and crime scene methodology through physical evidence, biological evidence, chemical analysis, anatomical analysis, and digital forensic disciplines. The learning graph shows how all 258 concepts relate and build on one another.

questions.question: What will I be able to do after completing this course?
questions.answer: After completing this course, students will be able to collect and analyze physical, biological, chemical, and digital evidence; perform calculations used in bloodstain pattern analysis, PMI estimation, and BAC retro-extrapolation; interpret laboratory outputs including DNA electropherograms and GC-MS chromatograms; and communicate findings in the language of expert testimony under the Daubert Standard.

questions.question: What topics are NOT covered in this course?
questions.answer: The course explicitly excludes: graphic autopsy photography; drug synthesis routes; offensive hacking techniques; methods for bypassing facial recognition; cell interception methods; unauthorized social media access; and graphic crime-scene imagery. Explosive chemistry is covered only at the post-blast diagnostic level.

questions.question: How does forensic science differ from what I see on TV crime shows?
questions.answer: TV crime dramas compress timelines, overstate certainty, and show unvalidated techniques. In reality, DNA analysis takes days to weeks, results are reported with statistical probabilities rather than absolute certainty, and all methods must meet Daubert Standard scrutiny for peer review and known error rates before being admitted in court.

questions.question: What is the Locard Exchange Principle and why is it so important?
questions.answer: The Locard Exchange Principle states that whenever two objects come into contact, each leaves trace material on the other. Every criminal leaves something at the scene and takes something away. This foundational principle justifies collecting hair, fiber, soil, glass, and other microscopic trace materials at crime scenes, as each type of contact creates a physical record of the interaction.

questions.question: What are the Daubert and Frye standards?
questions.answer: The Frye Standard (1923) requires that a scientific technique be 'generally accepted' in the relevant scientific community. The Daubert Standard (1993) requires federal judges to evaluate whether testimony is based on sufficient facts, reliable methodology, peer review, and known error rates. Most federal courts and many states use Daubert; some states still apply Frye.

questions.question: What is the difference between criminal law and civil law in a forensic context?
questions.answer: In criminal law, the government prosecutes offenses against society; the burden of proof is 'beyond a reasonable doubt.' In civil law, private parties seek remedies; the standard is 'preponderance of the evidence' (more likely than not). Forensic evidence that cannot convict in a criminal case may still be persuasive in a civil case due to the lower threshold.

questions.question: How many concepts does this course cover?
questions.answer: This course covers 258 concepts organized into 18 chapters across six thematic modules. These range from foundational ideas like the Locard Exchange Principle to advanced topics like convolutional neural networks in facial recognition and social media evidence authentication. The learning graph maps all dependencies between concepts.

questions.question: Does this course include interactive simulations?
questions.answer: Yes. The textbook includes browser-based MicroSims covering the forensic science history timeline, Locard Exchange Principle visualizer, Daubert vs. Frye decision workflow, and a criminal justice process flow diagram. These interactive tools run in any modern browser without special software.

questions.question: What is chain of custody and why does it matter?
questions.answer: Chain of custody is the documented, chronological record of who collected, handled, transferred, analyzed, and stored each piece of evidence from the crime scene through to the courtroom. Every transfer must be signed, dated, and recorded. A broken chain of custody can make evidence inadmissible or allow defense attorneys to argue tampering or contamination.

questions.question: What are the Seven S's of Crime Scene Investigation?
questions.answer: The Seven S's are: Securing the scene (establish perimeter), Separating witnesses and suspects, Scanning (visual survey before touching), Seeing (observe overall conditions), Sketching (measured drawing), Searching (systematic evidence search using a chosen pattern), and Securing evidence (collect, package, label, and log). Following this sequence protects evidence integrity.

questions.question: What is the difference between patent, plastic, and latent fingerprints?
questions.answer: Patent fingerprints are visible impressions left when a finger coated with blood, grease, or paint contacts a surface. Plastic fingerprints are three-dimensional impressions left in soft materials like wax or putty. Latent fingerprints are invisible impressions from natural oils and sweat that require chemical or physical development to visualize.

questions.question: What are the three major fingerprint pattern families?
questions.answer: The three major fingerprint patterns are: Loops (60–65% of the population) — ridges curve around a core and exit the same side; Whorls (30–35%) — ridges form closed circular or spiral patterns with two deltas; Arches (5%) — ridges enter from one side, rise in a wave, and exit the other side without recurving.

questions.question: What is the medullary index and how is it used to classify hair?
questions.answer: The medullary index is the ratio of medulla diameter to total hair diameter: medullary index = medulla diameter ÷ total hair diameter. A ratio below 0.33 is typical of human hair; above 0.50 indicates animal hair. This helps distinguish human from non-human hair before more expensive DNA analysis is performed.

questions.question: What is the refractive index and how is it used in glass analysis?
questions.answer: The refractive index (RI) measures how much a material slows and bends light. Each glass type has a characteristic RI based on its chemical composition. Forensic scientists use the Becke line test and immersion oil technique to measure RI to four decimal places, then compare glass fragments from a suspect to glass from a crime scene to assess common source.

questions.question: What is the difference between a presumptive test and a confirmatory test?
questions.answer: Presumptive tests are rapid screening tests that detect a broad class of substances; false positives can occur. Examples include the Kastle-Meyer test for blood. Confirmatory tests provide definitive identification using unique chemical properties and eliminate false positives. Examples include GC-MS for drugs and the Takayama test for blood. Both are required before courtroom evidence claims.

questions.question: How is the angle of impact of a bloodstain calculated?
questions.answer: The angle of impact is calculated using: angle = arcsin(width ÷ length). A circular stain indicates a 90-degree vertical drop. As the angle decreases, the stain becomes more elliptical. For example, a stain 4 mm wide and 8 mm long gives arcsin(0.5) = 30 degrees. Multiple angle calculations combined with area of convergence analysis determine the three-dimensional area of origin.

questions.question: What is PCR and why is it essential for forensic DNA profiling?
questions.answer: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) makes millions of copies of a specific DNA segment through repeated temperature cycling: denaturation (94–96°C separates DNA strands), annealing (50–65°C allows primers to bind), and extension (72°C synthesizes new strands). After 30 cycles, one molecule becomes over a billion copies, enabling analysis of tiny or degraded biological samples.

questions.question: What is the CODIS database and how is it used?
questions.answer: CODIS (Combined DNA Index System) is the FBI's national DNA database storing STR profiles from convicted offenders, arrestees, and crime scene evidence. All U.S. accredited labs use the same 20 core STR loci, enabling direct comparison across jurisdictions. Crime scene evidence profiles are searched against CODIS to identify suspects or link unsolved crimes.

questions.question: What does ADME stand for in forensic toxicology?
questions.answer: ADME describes the four pharmacokinetic stages: Absorption (drug enters bloodstream, rate depends on route), Distribution (drug moves from blood into tissues), Metabolism (liver converts drug into metabolites), and Elimination (kidneys remove drug and metabolites). ADME helps toxicologists determine when a drug was taken and whether it was at impairing concentrations at the time of an incident.

questions.question: What is the fire tetrahedron?
questions.answer: The fire tetrahedron represents four elements required for fire: fuel (combustible material), oxygen (oxidizing agent), heat (sufficient ignition energy), and chemical chain reaction (self-sustaining reaction). Removing any one element extinguishes the fire. Arson investigators use this model to interpret burn patterns and determine whether accelerants were deliberately introduced.

questions.question: What is a biological profile in forensic anthropology?
questions.answer: A biological profile is the set of key characteristics estimated from unidentified skeletal remains to guide identification: biological sex (from pelvic and cranial morphology), age at death (from epiphyseal fusion, pubic symphysis, cranial sutures), stature (from long bone regression equations), and ancestry (from craniometric analysis). The profile is combined with missing persons records to generate investigative leads.

questions.question: What is the post-mortem interval (PMI) and how is it estimated?
questions.answer: The post-mortem interval (PMI) is the time elapsed since death. Forensic entomologists estimate PMI using accumulated degree hours (ADH) — summing hourly temperatures above the insect species' minimum development threshold — and insect succession ecology, using the predictable sequence of insect species that colonize a body over time. Environmental variables must be factored into all calculations.

questions.question: What is gunshot residue (GSR) and what does it prove?
questions.answer: GSR consists of microscopic particles containing lead, barium, and antimony from primer combustion, deposited on the hands, face, and clothing of the shooter. SEM-EDX analysis detects these particles. GSR supports that a person recently fired or handled a discharged firearm, but results must be interpreted cautiously due to secondary transfer and dissipation over time.

questions.question: What does a questioned document examiner analyze?
questions.answer: Questioned document examiners analyze: handwriting comparison (letter form, slant, pressure, connecting strokes), ink analysis via TLC or LC-MS/MS, paper composition, and counterfeit detection. They determine whether a signature is genuine, whether a document was altered, and whether different sections were written with different inks or at different times.

questions.question: What is forensic imaging in digital forensics?
questions.answer: Forensic imaging creates an exact, sector-by-sector bit-stream copy of a digital storage device, capturing active files, deleted files, unallocated space, and slack space. A write-blocker prevents any modification of the original. Hash values (MD5 or SHA-256) are calculated before and after imaging to verify the copy is unaltered. All analysis is performed on the image.

questions.question: How does facial recognition technology work in law enforcement?
questions.answer: Facial recognition uses convolutional neural networks (CNNs) that detect a face, align it to standard position using landmark points (eyes, nose, mouth), extract a numerical feature vector, and compare that vector to database profiles using distance metrics. Law enforcement uses databases like NGI-Facial. Results are a ranked candidate list evaluated by a trained examiner, not an automatic identification.

questions.question: What is a Call Detail Record (CDR) and what does it contain?
questions.answer: A Call Detail Record (CDR) is generated by a carrier for every call, text, and data session. Key fields include phone number (MSISDN), device identifier (IMEI), date and time, call duration, originating and terminating tower IDs, and tower sector. CDR analysis reconstructs where a mobile device was geographically located when communications occurred.
concepts: Cell Phone Analytics and Mobile Forensics

questions.question: What is OSINT and how is it used in forensic investigations?
questions.answer: OSINT (Open-Source Intelligence) is the collection and analysis of publicly available information — including social media, public records, and online databases — for investigative purposes. Forensic investigators use OSINT to locate suspects, establish timelines, geolocate individuals from post metadata, and map social networks. All collection must comply with Fourth Amendment constraints and platform Terms of Service.
concepts: Social Media Analysis and Open-Source Intelligence

questions.question: What is cyanoacrylate fuming and when is it used?
questions.answer: Cyanoacrylate fuming (super glue fuming) releases vapors in a sealed chamber that react with amino acids, fatty acids, and water in fingerprint residue to deposit a white polymer on friction ridges. It is most effective on non-porous surfaces such as plastic bags, glass, and metal. The resulting print can be further enhanced with fluorescent dyes.

questions.question: What is ninhydrin and what surfaces is it used on?
questions.answer: Ninhydrin is a chemical reagent that reacts with amino acids in sweat residue to produce a dark purple compound called Ruhemann's purple. It is used on porous surfaces such as paper, cardboard, and unfinished wood. It is particularly effective for developing aged fingerprints because amino acids are relatively stable and do not evaporate quickly.

questions.question: What is the difference between class evidence and individual evidence?
questions.answer: Class evidence identifies the general group a sample came from (fiber type, blood group, paint color) but cannot link it to a single source. Individual evidence can be uniquely linked to a specific source with high scientific certainty — examples include DNA STR profiles, fingerprints with sufficient minutiae, and firearm striation marks. Strong cases combine both types.

questions.question: What is the 3R Rule in glass fracture analysis?
questions.answer: The 3R Rule: Radial fractures form a Right angle on the Reverse side from the force. Radial fractures (cracks extending outward from impact) have Wallner lines whose right angle points toward the force direction. When a pane is struck multiple times, existing fracture lines stop later cracks, allowing investigators to determine the sequence of impacts.

questions.question: What does GC-MS stand for and why is it the gold standard for drug identification?
questions.answer: GC-MS stands for Gas Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry. GC separates mixture components by chemical properties; MS identifies each component by its unique molecular fragment mass spectrum — a molecular fingerprint. Together they provide both quantification (how much) and definitive identification (which substance), meeting the highest standards for admissible forensic evidence.

questions.question: What are the five stages of decomposition?
questions.answer: The five stages are: Fresh (minimal external change, internal microbial activity begins); Bloat (gas accumulation from anaerobic bacteria, strong odor); Active decay (rapid tissue loss from microbes and larvae, decomposition fluids); Advanced decay (primarily dry skin, cartilage, reduced insect activity); and Dry remains (bone, dried skin, hair — may persist for years).

questions.question: What are STRs and why are they used in forensic DNA profiling?
questions.answer: Short Tandem Repeats (STRs) are genomic locations where a short DNA sequence is repeated a variable number of times. Each person has two alleles (inherited from each parent) at each STR locus. STRs are highly variable between individuals, can be amplified from trace samples by PCR, and the combined probability of a random 20-locus CODIS profile match is typically less than one in a quadrillion.

questions.question: What is capillary electrophoresis and how is it used to read a DNA profile?
questions.answer: Capillary electrophoresis (CE) separates PCR-amplified STR fragments by size through a polymer-filled capillary using an electric field. Smaller fragments travel faster. A laser detector records each fragment as a fluorescent peak. The resulting electropherogram shows peaks whose positions (sizes) identify alleles at each STR locus. Analysts read peak positions to assemble the DNA profile.

questions.question: What is BAC retro-extrapolation and when is it used?
questions.answer: BAC retro-extrapolation estimates a person's blood alcohol concentration at an earlier time based on a later blood draw, using the average elimination rate of 0.015 g/dL per hour. For example, a BAC of 0.06 g/dL measured 2 hours after a crash gives an estimated 0.09 g/dL at the time of the crash. Results are reported with uncertainty ranges because individual elimination rates vary.

questions.question: What is headspace analysis and how does it detect arson accelerants?
questions.answer: Fire debris is sealed in an airtight container and gently heated, causing volatile hydrocarbon compounds from accelerant residues to evaporate into the headspace above the debris. The vapor is sampled (via SPME or syringe) and injected into a GC-MS instrument. The chromatographic pattern of separated hydrocarbons is compared to known accelerant profiles to identify gasoline, diesel, or other accelerants.

questions.question: What is epiphyseal fusion and how does it help estimate age?
questions.answer: Epiphyses are cartilaginous growth plates at the ends of long bones that ossify and fuse to the bone shaft at predictable ages during adolescence and young adulthood. Elbow epiphyses fuse in the early-to-mid teens; the medial clavicle — the last — fuses between ages 22 and 30. Fusion status allows forensic anthropologists to estimate age in subadults and young adults.

questions.question: What is lands and grooves rifling and how does it help identify firearms?
questions.answer: Rifling is the spiral pattern of raised ridges (lands) and recessed channels (grooves) inside a firearm barrel that imparts spin to bullets. As a bullet passes through, lands leave unique striation marks on the bullet's surface. Firearms examiners use a comparison microscope to view striations from a crime scene bullet side-by-side with test-fire bullets from a suspect weapon.

questions.question: What is EXIF metadata and what can it reveal?
questions.answer: EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format) metadata is automatically embedded in digital photos, including date and time, GPS coordinates (if enabled), camera or phone model, and lens settings. In forensic investigations, EXIF data can place a device at a specific location and time, link photos to a specific camera, and contradict a suspect's alibi. Many social media platforms strip EXIF data on upload.

questions.question: What is cell tower triangulation?
questions.answer: Cell tower triangulation estimates a mobile device's geographic location by analyzing signal data from three or more nearby towers. The timing of signal arrival at multiple towers allows calculation of the device's approximate distance from each. Intersecting the distance circles from three or more towers produces a geographic area where the device was located. Precision varies from hundreds of meters in urban areas to several kilometers rurally.
concepts: Cell Phone Analytics and Mobile Forensics

questions.question: What are the three legal mechanisms for obtaining social media records?
questions.answer: The three mechanisms are: Preservation Letter (voluntary request to freeze records for 90 days while legal process is obtained), Subpoena (compels basic subscriber information and limited transactional records), and Court Order or Search Warrant (required for content data such as message contents and stored photos — requires probable cause). Higher-sensitivity data requires higher legal authority.
concepts: Social Media Analysis and Open-Source Intelligence

questions.question: Why can a broken chain of custody destroy an otherwise strong case?
questions.answer: A documented gap in chain of custody gives defense attorneys grounds to argue the evidence could have been tampered with, contaminated, or substituted. Judges may suppress the evidence entirely. Even if the evidence is admitted, the gap allows defense counsel to create reasonable doubt in the jury's mind. Every transfer — however minor — must be documented.

questions.question: Why can't luminol results alone confirm the presence of blood?
questions.answer: Luminol is a presumptive test that reacts with the peroxidase-like activity of iron in hemoglobin, but bleach, copper, and certain plant extracts produce the same reaction. A positive luminol result indicates blood may be present, not that blood is present. A confirmatory test such as the Takayama crystal test or Kastle-Meyer test is required before any courtroom claim. Luminol can also partially degrade DNA.

questions.question: What makes DNA evidence from mixed samples difficult to interpret?
questions.answer: Mixed DNA samples contain genetic material from two or more individuals. Challenges include stochastic effects (allele drop-out in low-template samples), minor contributor alleles masked by major contributor peaks, stutter artifacts that can mimic real alleles, and exponential combinatorial complexity when three or more contributors are present. Mixture interpretation requires specialized software, validated statistical models, and expert training.

questions.question: Why do GSR results need to be interpreted cautiously?
questions.answer: GSR can transfer secondarily from a shooter to bystanders through physical contact or from surfaces (car seats, handcuffs) to people who never fired. Certain occupational exposures (lead paint) produce similar particles. GSR dissipates over time through hand movement and washing — a negative result hours after a shooting does not prove the person did not fire. GSR is most valuable when paired with other corroborating evidence.

questions.question: How can environmental conditions affect forensic entomology PMI estimates?
questions.answer: Insect development rates are temperature-dependent. Errors arise from: delayed colonization if the body was moved or covered (the entomological clock starts later than death); temperature microclimate differences between the body location and the nearest weather station; and using development rate data from the wrong geographic population of a species. Forensic entomologists report a minimum PMI, not a precise time of death.

questions.question: What is the difference between antemortem, perimortem, and postmortem bone trauma?
questions.answer: Antemortem trauma occurred before death and shows healing evidence (bone callus, periosteal reaction). Perimortem trauma occurred at or near death when bone was still fresh — it shows plastic deformation and irregular fracture edges typical of green bone. Postmortem trauma occurred after death; dry bone fractures with sharp, angular breaks and bleached edges. Accurate differentiation prevents false inferences about cause of death.

questions.question: Why is facial recognition evidence controversial in court?
questions.answer: Facial recognition evidence faces scrutiny because: documented demographic bias shows higher false positive rates for darker-skinned individuals and women; performance degrades with non-frontal poses and poor camera quality; there are no standardized national error rate benchmarks; and human examiners introduce subjective judgment when reviewing candidate lists. Defendants have successfully challenged facial recognition identifications under Daubert scrutiny.

questions.question: What makes digital evidence authentication challenging?
questions.answer: Digital evidence authentication challenges include: timestamps can be manipulated; metadata can be altered with freely available tools; screenshots can be doctored; social media platforms often strip EXIF data on upload. Federal Rule of Evidence 901 requires demonstrating the evidence is what it purports to be. Hash value verification, full-page captures, and URL documentation are required for robust authentication.

questions.question: Why does ink chromatography matter in document examination?
questions.answer: When a document is altered — such as a forged signature added to a will or a changed date on a contract — the forger typically uses a pen with different ink than the original author. Thin-layer chromatography (TLC) separates ink dye components by Rf value. Different formulations produce different patterns, revealing alterations. TLC can also show that a specific ink was not commercially available before a claimed date.

questions.question: What search pattern should be used and when?
questions.answer: Grid pattern (two perpendicular overlapping search lines) is most thorough and best for large outdoor scenes. Spiral pattern works well for smaller scenes with a clear center. Zone pattern divides a large indoor scene into sectors for multiple searchers. Regardless of pattern, searchers should document their path, mark items with numbered placards, and photograph before collection.

questions.question: How should biological evidence be packaged and why?
questions.answer: Biological evidence must be packaged in paper, not plastic. Paper allows air circulation, preventing moisture buildup that promotes bacterial growth and degrades DNA. Items should be air-dried before packaging when possible, collected with sterile tools, placed in biohazard-labeled containers, and sealed with tamper-evident seals. Each item receives a unique evidence number and is logged immediately.

questions.question: When should a forensic scientist use a write-blocker when examining digital evidence?
questions.answer: A write-blocker must be used every time a digital storage device is connected to an examination computer before forensic imaging. Write-blockers intercept any write commands to the connected device, preventing modification. Without one, simply connecting a drive to Windows can alter timestamps and directory structures, invalidating hash verification and potentially making evidence inadmissible.

questions.question: How should a forensic scientist communicate limitations of evidence to a jury?
questions.answer: Expert witnesses must state the method and its known error rate, distinguish between a 'match' and a definitive 'identification,' avoid absolute language unless scientifically justified, disclose factors affecting reliability (sample degradation, mixture complexity), and answer cross-examination honestly including acknowledging valid alternative explanations. Overstating certainty has contributed to wrongful convictions.

questions.question: What factors should guide the choice of fingerprint development technique?
questions.answer: The substrate is the primary factor. Porous surfaces (paper, wood) — use ninhydrin, DFO, or silver nitrate. Non-porous surfaces (glass, plastic, metal) — use aluminum powder, cyanoacrylate fuming, or rhodamine dye. Wet surfaces — use small particle reagent. Multi-colored surfaces — use luminescent techniques. Sequence matters: use luminescent dyes before powders, cyanoacrylate before dyes.

questions.question: How should a forensic entomologist collect insect evidence from a scene?
questions.answer: Collect larvae in two batches: one preserved immediately in 70–80% ethanol (to document current stage), one transported alive for laboratory rearing and species identification. Record ambient and microhabitat temperature with a calibrated thermometer, note larval instar stage in the field, photograph insect activity before collecting, and collect empty pupal cases as evidence of completed development cycles.

questions.question: How can an investigator corroborate a suspect's claimed location using digital evidence?
questions.answer: Multiple sources should be cross-referenced: cell tower CDR records, GPS device history, Wi-Fi connection logs (which place a device at a precise access point), social media post timestamps and geotagged photos, EXIF metadata from unedited photos, and financial transaction records (ATM withdrawals, credit card swipes). No single source is sufficient — independent corroboration strengthens the evidence significantly.

questions.question: How is the random match probability calculated for a DNA profile?
questions.answer: The product rule multiplies the frequency of each observed allele combination across all STR loci, using population databases. Because CODIS loci are on different chromosomes or far apart, they are inherited independently. With 20 CODIS loci, the combined random match probability is typically smaller than one in one trillion, making coincidental matches essentially impossible. Results must be reported with the population reference database used.

questions.question: How does the Fourth Amendment apply to digital searches?
questions.answer: The Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable digital searches. Key precedents include Riley v. California (2014) requiring warrants for cell phone contents after arrest. The third-party doctrine provides less protection for records shared with carriers (CDRs). Cloud content typically requires a warrant or court order. Border searches have broader authority. Evidence collected in violation of the Fourth Amendment may be suppressed.

questions.question: What is the Daubert Standard analysis for facial recognition evidence?
questions.answer: Under Daubert, a judge evaluates facial recognition evidence on: testing and known error rate (has it been empirically tested?), peer review and publication, documented false positive rates including demographic disparities, and general acceptance in the scientific community. Defense challenges typically focus on demographic bias (higher false positive rates for some ethnic and gender groups) and the subjectivity of human examiner final calls.

questions.question: How does steganography challenge digital forensics?
questions.answer: Steganography hides data within ordinary-looking files (typically images) by altering pixel values in ways invisible to casual inspection. Forensic challenges include: hidden data does not appear in normal keyword searches; specialized steganalysis software is required to detect statistical anomalies; hidden content may also be encrypted; and without knowing the specific algorithm, extraction may be impossible.

questions.question: What are the main methods for recovering deleted data from mobile devices?
questions.answer: Mobile forensic acquisition methods include: Logical acquisition (active files only, fast), File system acquisition (includes marked-deleted files), Physical acquisition (full bit-by-bit image of flash memory including unallocated space), and Chip-off acquisition (direct reading of physically removed memory chip — last resort for damaged/locked devices). Tools like Cellebrite UFED support multiple methods. Method choice depends on device model, OS, encryption, and goals.
concepts: Cell Phone Analytics and Mobile Forensics

questions.question: How are stature regression equations used in forensic anthropology?
questions.answer: Stature regression equations predict living height from long bone length (femur, tibia, humerus, etc.) using the formula: Estimated stature = (bone length × coefficient) + constant ± standard error. Different equations exist for different populations, sexes, and bones. Results are reported as a range (typically ±3–5 cm) and combined with other biological profile elements to guide identification efforts.

questions.question: What is social network analysis and how is it applied in criminal investigations?
questions.answer: Social network analysis (SNA) maps relationships and communication patterns between individuals to identify key actors (high betweenness centrality — brokers between groups), dense communication clusters, information flow direction, and intermediaries. Applied to CDRs, financial transactions, and social media connections, SNA reveals organizational structure in criminal networks. Visualized as graphs with nodes (individuals) and edges (connections).
concepts: Social Media Analysis and Open-Source Intelligence
